one_line_takeaway: CoRIT shows that CLIP-based deepfake detectors trained with SAM can collapse to chance-level performance on subtle, non-semantic forgeries once the SAM perturbation radius crosses a theoretically-derived Critical Optimization Radius tied to degraded Gradient Signal-to-Noise Ratio, and that re-injecting high-GSNR regional signal into an otherwise frozen CLIP backbone restores state-of-the-art cross-domain generalization (88.9% avg frame-level AUC) at roughly 90% lower training memory than PEFT alternatives.
key_finding: Under the officially recommended SAM radius (rho=0.05), a frozen-CLIP-ViT-L/14 linear-probe detector converges normally on DeepFakes/FaceSwap/Face2Face but collapses to random guessing on NeuralTextures; effective learning on NT is only restored at rho=0.01, empirically demonstrating a forgery-type-dependent Critical Optimization Radius. Layer-wise analysis (Table VII) shows the UniFD baseline's COR on NT falls below 0.05 by layer 15 and reaches 0.01 at the final layer (Collapse Zone), whereas CoRIT keeps final-layer COR at 0.38. On classical cross-domain benchmarks (Table I, frame-level AUC), CoRIT reaches an average of 88.9% (CDFv1 90.9, CDFv2 89.1, DFDCP 88.8, DFDC 84.5, DFD 91.3), beating the frozen-backbone baseline UniFD (avg 76.9%) by 12.0 points, the PEFT baseline ForAda (avg 83.8%) by 5.1 points, and the CNN-based FIA-USA by 9.2 points on DFD specifically. Video-level average AUC (Table II) reaches 91.0%. On the DF40 next-generation benchmark (Table III) CoRIT beats FIA-USA by 4.4% and UniFD by 11.6% on average. On UniversalFakeDetect (Tables IV/V) CoRIT reaches 97.17% mAP, beating NPR by 4.41 mAP/2.28 mAcc, FreqNet by 5.52 mAP/4.75 mAcc, and UniFD by 7.03 mAP/8.46 mAcc. Cross-manipulation leave-one-out evaluation (Table VI) shows CoRIT improving over UniFD by 12.5% on F2F and 8.2% on NT. The ablation (Table VIII) shows the component contributions on a 4-dataset average AUC: baseline 78.6% -> +RSI 83.8% -> +RSI+RRM 86.7% -> +HRI alone 82.3% -> full CoRIT (RSI+RRM+HRI) 88.8%. Because the backbone stays frozen, training GPU memory drops from 21,224 MB (ForAda, PEFT) to 2,130 MB (CoRIT), a 90% reduction.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and theoretically formalizes a failure mode called 'Optimization Collapse' in CLIP-based deepfake detectors trained with Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM): detection AUC collapses to near-random on non-semantic (subtle) forgeries once the SAM perturbation radius rho exceeds a per-forgery-type threshold. The authors define the Critical Optimization Radius (COR) to quantify this threshold, prove a theorem ('COR-GSNR Stability Decomposition') that COR increases monotonically with Gradient Signal-to-Noise Ratio (GSNR), and use this theory to motivate CoRIT (Contrastive Regional Injection Transformer): a frozen-CLIP-backbone architecture that uses a lightweight Contrastive Gradient Proxy (CGP) plus three training-free modules -- Region Refinement Mask (suppresses CGP variance), Regional Signal Injection (counteracts layer-wise attenuation of non-semantic signal by re-injecting high-GSNR regional features across layers), and Hierarchical Representation Integration (fuses an intermediate layer's representation with the final layer's, concatenating layer-18 and layer-24 outputs per their own ablation) -- to raise COR out of the collapse zone and improve cross-domain generalization.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model in the classical AI-security sense. The 'threat' modeled is the generic arms race between generative synthesis (GANs, diffusion models, face-swapping/reenactment pipelines) producing increasingly hyper-realistic, semantically-imperceptible forged facial and natural-scene content, and forensic classifiers trying to generalize to unseen generators. The paper does not model an adaptive attacker who deliberately perturbs images to evade the detector; its 'Robustness Analysis' (Section V-F) instead evaluates resilience to four benign, non-adversarial image degradations -- Gaussian Blur, Block-wise Distortion, Contrast Change, and JPEG Compression -- via a Relative AUC Variation (R_AUC) metric, not an adversarial-evasion or poisoning threat model.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion section (VI-B) states that current forensic benchmarks are dominated by high-COR (semantically overt) forgery types such as Celeb-DF and uniface, while low-COR (non-semantic) forgeries like NeuralTextures/Face2Face -- the hardest cases the paper's theory targets -- remain underrepresented; the authors frame this as an open benchmark-design gap rather than something CoRIT resolves. In the cross-manipulation evaluation (Table VI), CoRIT still performs relatively worse on F2F and NT than on the coarser DF/FS forgeries, i.e., the semantic/non-semantic generalization gap is narrowed, not closed. Robustness testing (Fig. 8) is limited to four benign, non-adversarial image degradations (blur, block-wise distortion, contrast change, JPEG compression); no evaluation against an adaptive adversary that deliberately perturbs inputs to evade the detector. CoRIT shows higher sensitivity than baselines specifically to Block-wise Distortion, which the authors attribute to disruption of CLIP's semantic-integrity prior by the masking-like occlusion. The Region Refinement Mask / Region Token design (Table IX: Foreground/Boundary/Background regions) is built around facial forgery structure; its extension to the natural-scene UniversalFakeDetect benchmark is evaluated but the architecture's region-based inductive bias originates from facial forensics.

datasets_used: DF40 (next-generation face-forgery benchmark: facedancer, blendface, e4s, danet, uniface, fsgan, etc.)
UniversalFakeDetect benchmark (natural-scene GAN/diffusion images sourced from ImageNet/LAION)
quantitative_results: Table I frame-level AUC average: CoRIT 88.9% vs UniFD (frozen-backbone baseline) 76.9% vs ForAda (PEFT adapter) 83.8%; CoRIT beats FIA-USA by 9.2% on DFD specifically
Table I per-dataset frame-level AUC for CoRIT: CDFv1 90.9%, CDFv2 89.1%, DFDCP 88.8%, DFDC 84.5%, DFD 91.3%
Table II video-level average AUC: CoRIT 91.0%
Table III (DF40 next-gen benchmarks): CoRIT beats FIA-USA by 4.4% average AUC and UniFD by 11.6% average AUC

Tables IV/V (UniversalFakeDetect): CoRIT 97.17% mAP; beats NPR by 4.41% mAP / 2.28% mAcc; beats FreqNet by 5.52% mAP / 4.75% mAcc; beats UniFD by 7.03% mAP / 8.46% mAcc; 97.35% mAcc on perceptual-loss-generated forgeries
Table VI cross-manipulation leave-one-out: CoRIT improves over UniFD by 12.5% AUC on F2F and 8.2% AUC on NT
Table VII layer-wise COR on NT: UniFD COR drops below 0.05 by layer 15 and to 0.01 at layer 24 (Collapse Zone); CoRIT final-layer COR remains 0.38
Table VIII ablation, 4-dataset avg frame-level AUC: no components 78.6% -> +RSI 83.8% -> +RSI+RRM 86.7% -> +HRI only 82.3% -> full CoRIT (RSI+RRM+HRI) 88.8%
Table IX region ablation: Foreground-only region (K=1) avg AUC 86.8% vs Random region avg 80.7%
Table XII intermediate-layer fusion ablation: best average AUC 88.9% using layer 18 for the intermediate feature
Table XIII fusion architecture ablation: Concatenation 88.9% avg AUC vs Cross-Attention 88.0% avg AUC (0.9-point gap)
Computational efficiency: training GPU memory reduced from 21,224 MB (ForAda) to 2,130 MB (CoRIT), a 90% reduction

Fig. 8 robustness (Relative AUC Variation): CoRIT shows the slowest AUC degradation among compared methods under heavy JPEG compression (levels 1-5), but higher sensitivity than baselines under Block-wise Distortion
